---
title: "NSQL Extension 01: Graph Theory"
subtitle: "Extending Natural Structured Query Language with graph theory terminology"
chapter: "CH00"
category: "extension"
number: "NSQL_EXT01"
date-created: "2025-08-23"
date-modified: "2025-08-23"
author: "Claude"
type: "language-extension"
law: "Terminology Standards"
article-number: "Extension 1"
related_to:
  - "MP56_connected_component_principle"
  - "MP63_graph_theory_in_nsql"
  - "MP024_natural_sql_language"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: false
    code-tools: true
    number-sections: true
---

# Extension 1: Graph Theory in NSQL {#overview}

## Section 1: Introduction {#introduction}

This document extends the Natural Structured Query Language (NSQL) with graph theory terminology to support the connected component principle (MP56) and enable formal reasoning about application structure through graph-based concepts.

## Section 2: Type Definitions {#type-definitions}

### Subsection 1: Basic Graph Types {#basic-types}

```nsql
TYPE Vertex = Element ID and properties
TYPE Edge = Connection between vertices with optional direction and weight
TYPE Graph = Collection of vertices and edges
TYPE Path = Ordered sequence of vertices connected by edges
TYPE Component = Subgraph where all vertices are connected by some path
```

### Subsection 2: Extended Types {#extended-types}

```nsql
TYPE DirectedGraph = Graph where edges have direction
TYPE UndirectedGraph = Graph where edges are bidirectional
TYPE WeightedGraph = Graph where edges have numeric weights
TYPE Tree = Connected acyclic graph
TYPE DAG = Directed Acyclic Graph
TYPE ConnectedComponent = Maximal connected subgraph
```

## Section 3: Graph Definition Syntax {#graph-syntax}

### Subsection 1: Basic Graph Definition {#basic-graph}

```nsql
GRAPH(name) {
  VERTICES {
    name: properties,
    ...
  }
  EDGES {
    source -> destination [: properties],
    ...
  }
}
```

### Subsection 2: Example Graph Definition {#graph-example}

```nsql
GRAPH(app_structure) {
  VERTICES {
    ui_component: {type: "UI", module: "microCustomer"},
    server_component: {type: "Server", module: "microCustomer"},
    data_connection: {type: "Data", source: "database"}
  }
  EDGES {
    ui_component -> server_component: {interaction: "reactive"},
    server_component -> data_connection: {query: "SQL"}
  }
}
```

## Section 4: Component Operations {#component-operations}

### Subsection 1: Component Identification {#component-id}

```nsql
COMPONENT(component_name) FROM graph_name {
  ENTRY_POINTS: [vertex_ids]
  EXIT_POINTS: [vertex_ids]
  INTERNAL_VERTICES: [vertex_ids]
}
```

### Subsection 2: Component Analysis {#component-analysis}

```nsql
ANALYZE COMPONENT component_name {
  CONNECTIVITY: strongly_connected | weakly_connected
  CYCLES: detect_cycles()
  CRITICAL_PATHS: find_critical_paths()
  BOTTLENECKS: identify_bottlenecks()
}
```

## Section 5: Path Expressions {#path-expressions}

### Subsection 1: Path Definition {#path-definition}

```nsql
PATH(path_name) FROM source TO destination {
  MAX_LENGTH: n
  AVOID: [vertex_ids]
  THROUGH: [required_vertex_ids]
  WEIGHT: minimize | maximize
}
```

### Subsection 2: Path Queries {#path-queries}

```nsql
FIND ALL PATHS FROM vertex_a TO vertex_b
FIND SHORTEST PATH FROM vertex_a TO vertex_b
FIND PATHS WITH LENGTH <= n
FIND CRITICAL PATH IN component
```

## Section 6: Graph Queries {#graph-queries}

### Subsection 1: Connectivity Queries {#connectivity}

```nsql
IS_CONNECTED(graph_name) -> BOOLEAN
FIND_COMPONENTS(graph_name) -> LIST[Component]
IS_REACHABLE(vertex_a, vertex_b) -> BOOLEAN
DEGREE(vertex) -> INTEGER
```

### Subsection 2: Structural Queries {#structural}

```nsql
HAS_CYCLE(graph_name) -> BOOLEAN
IS_TREE(graph_name) -> BOOLEAN
IS_DAG(graph_name) -> BOOLEAN
TOPOLOGICAL_SORT(graph_name) -> LIST[Vertex]
```

## Section 7: Application Structure Modeling {#app-modeling}

### Subsection 1: Module Dependency Graph {#module-graph}

```nsql
GRAPH(module_dependencies) {
  VERTICES {
    FOR EACH module IN application {
      module_id: {name: module.name, type: module.type}
    }
  }
  EDGES {
    FOR EACH dependency IN module.dependencies {
      module -> dependency: {type: "requires"}
    }
  }
}
```

### Subsection 2: Data Flow Graph {#data-flow}

```nsql
GRAPH(data_flow) {
  VERTICES {
    FOR EACH component IN ui_components {
      component_id: {type: "UI", reactive: true}
    }
    FOR EACH table IN database {
      table_id: {type: "Data", mutable: true}
    }
  }
  EDGES {
    FOR EACH data_binding IN bindings {
      source -> destination: {flow: "unidirectional"}
    }
  }
}
```

## Section 8: Graph Algorithms {#algorithms}

### Subsection 1: Search Algorithms {#search}

```nsql
BFS(graph, start_vertex) -> LIST[Vertex]
DFS(graph, start_vertex) -> LIST[Vertex]
DIJKSTRA(graph, source, destination) -> Path
A_STAR(graph, source, destination, heuristic) -> Path
```

### Subsection 2: Analysis Algorithms {#analysis}

```nsql
STRONGLY_CONNECTED_COMPONENTS(graph) -> LIST[Component]
MINIMUM_SPANNING_TREE(graph) -> Tree
MAXIMUM_FLOW(graph, source, sink) -> NUMBER
PAGE_RANK(graph) -> MAP[Vertex, NUMBER]
```

## Section 9: Practical Examples {#examples}

### Subsection 1: Component Dependency Analysis {#dependency-example}

```nsql
// Find all components that depend on a specific module
QUERY component_dependencies {
  GRAPH(app_structure)
  FIND ALL PATHS TO module_id("database_connection")
  RETURN UNIQUE VERTICES FROM PATHS
}
```

### Subsection 2: Circular Dependency Detection {#circular-example}

```nsql
// Detect circular dependencies in module structure
QUERY circular_dependencies {
  GRAPH(module_dependencies)
  FOR EACH component IN FIND_COMPONENTS(module_dependencies) {
    IF HAS_CYCLE(component) {
      RETURN component WITH cycle_path
    }
  }
}
```

### Subsection 3: Critical Path Identification {#critical-path}

```nsql
// Find critical initialization path
QUERY initialization_critical_path {
  GRAPH(initialization_sequence)
  FIND CRITICAL PATH FROM "start" TO "app_ready"
  RETURN path WITH total_time
}
```

## Section 10: Integration with NSQL Core {#integration}

### Subsection 1: Graph-SQL Bridge {#graph-sql}

```nsql
// Convert relational data to graph
CREATE GRAPH FROM TABLE relationships {
  VERTICES: SELECT id, properties FROM entities
  EDGES: SELECT source_id, target_id, relationship_type FROM relationships
}

// Query graph and return as table
SELECT * FROM GRAPH_QUERY(module_dependencies) {
  FIND_COMPONENTS() AS component_id,
  COUNT(VERTICES) AS component_size
}
```

### Subsection 2: Hybrid Queries {#hybrid}

```nsql
// Combine graph and relational operations
QUERY hybrid_analysis {
  components = FIND_COMPONENTS(GRAPH(app_structure))
  FOR EACH component IN components {
    metrics = SELECT COUNT(*), AVG(complexity) 
              FROM modules 
              WHERE module_id IN component.vertices
    RETURN component WITH metrics
  }
}
```

---
*This extension provides comprehensive graph theory support in NSQL, enabling sophisticated structural analysis and reasoning about application architecture.*
